title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Quiet Power of Failure
content.subtitle: How the world's most successful innovators learned to reframe setback as essential data.
content.text: There is a peculiar irony at the heart of modern innovation culture. We celebrate success loudly, publishing it in glossy case studies and keynote speeches, while quietly burying the long, messy trail of failures that almost always preceded it. This selective narrative does a profound disservice to anyone trying to build something new. [31] The writer argues that by hiding failure, we have constructed a dangerously false impression of how progress actually works—and inadvertently made the fear of failure far more paralysing than it needs to be. [/31] In reality, failure is not the opposite of success; it is, for most innovators, its most indispensable ingredient. || The scientific method itself is built on failure. A hypothesis is not proved by a single successful experiment; it is refined through a succession of experiments that reveal what does not work. [32] Thomas Edison's famous remark—that he had not failed but had simply 'found ten thousand ways that won't work'—captures this mindset perfectly: each failed attempt was not a defeat but a data point that narrowed the territory of the unknown. [/32] This reframing, from failure as verdict to failure as information, is the cognitive shift that separates serial innovators from those who give up at the first obstacle. || [33] The author's tone when discussing organisations that punish failure is one of measured but clear criticism. [/33] In many corporate environments, the incentive structure is designed to protect the individual from being associated with any outcome that falls short of success. Employees learn quickly to avoid risky projects, to hedge their commitments, and to attribute failures to external factors. The result is a culture of 'safe mediocrity' in which genuine innovation is structurally impossible. Real progress requires permission to fail, and that permission must come from the top. || However, not all failure is equally valuable. [34] Researchers in the field of organisational learning distinguish between 'productive failure,' which generates new understanding, and 'preventable failure,' which results from inattention, poor process, or a lack of basic competence. [/34] Celebrating all failure indiscriminately, as some popular business philosophies seem to do, is as misguided as condemning it entirely. The goal is not to fail often; it is to fail well—to extract the maximum amount of usable knowledge from each setback before moving on. || [35] On a personal level, our relationship with failure is shaped very early and very powerfully by the educational systems in which we are raised. [/35] Most school environments reward correct answers and penalise mistakes, reinforcing the belief that being wrong is shameful. By the time students enter the professional world, many carry a deeply ingrained terror of being seen to fail, which is entirely at odds with the mindset required for creative and entrepreneurial work. Reforming how schools frame and respond to error may, in the long run, be the most impactful change we can make to a society's capacity for innovation. || In the end, perhaps the most honest thing we can say about failure is that it is simply the price of ambition. [36] The writer concludes that those who refuse to risk failure are not protecting themselves; they are simply choosing a different, quieter, and ultimately less interesting kind of failure—the failure to try. [/36] The only truly wasted failure is the one from which nothing is learned, and the only truly wasted life is the one in which nothing difficult was ever attempted. Embracing failure is not recklessness; it is, in the deepest sense, the most serious form of optimism.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, what does the author suggest is the consequence of hiding failure?
options: A) It motivates people to work harder in order to achieve visible success.
B) It creates a distorted view of how innovation works, making failure seem worse.
C) It protects young innovators from discouragement at a critical stage.
D) It ensures that only the most important successes are remembered.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31] and the idea that hiding failure creates 'a dangerously false impression' and makes 'the fear of failure far more paralysing.' A, C, and D are not the author's argument.

questions.question: How does the author use Edison's remark to support the argument in the second paragraph?
options: A) To show that persistence alone is enough to guarantee eventual success.
B) To illustrate that repeated failure should lead to abandoning a project.
C) To demonstrate how reframing failure as useful data drives innovation.
D) To argue that laboratory experiments are the best model for all businesses.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the description of each failed attempt as 'a data point that narrowed the territory of the unknown.' A and B misrepresent the author's point. D is not suggested.

questions.question: What does the author say happens when organisations punish failure?
options: A) Employees become more focused and produce higher quality results.
B) The organisation attracts only the most competent and cautious workers.
C) Workers avoid risk, making genuine innovation structurally impossible.
D) Leaders gain better control over the direction of new projects.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the description of a 'culture of safe mediocrity in which genuine innovation is structurally impossible.' A, B, and D are not suggested and contradict the author's view.

questions.question: What distinction do researchers make regarding types of failure, according to the fourth paragraph?
options: A) Between failure that happens quickly and failure that develops slowly over time.
B) Between failure in large organisations and failure in small start-ups.
C) Between failure that teaches something useful and failure caused by negligence.
D) Between personal failure and professional failure in the workplace.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the contrast between 'productive failure,' which 'generates new understanding,' and 'preventable failure,' which results from 'inattention, poor process, or a lack of basic competence.' A, B, and D are not the distinctions described.

questions.question: What does the author suggest about the role of education in our attitude toward failure?
options: A) Schools should stop setting tests so that students can learn without fear.
B) Educational reform could be one of the most powerful ways to improve innovation.
C) The current school system is the only reason adults are afraid of failure.
D) Students today are taught to be more resilient than previous generations.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the claim that reforming schools may be 'the most impactful change we can make to a society's capacity for innovation.' A is too extreme. C overstates the argument. D contradicts the author's view.

questions.question: What does the writer mean by calling the refusal to try 'a different kind of failure'?
options: A) That caution is always preferable to reckless ambition.
B) That not attempting anything difficult is itself a form of loss.
C) That small, safe projects are as valuable as large, risky ones.
D) That failure should be avoided at all costs to protect one's reputation.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [36] and the idea that refusing to risk failure is 'a different, quieter, and ultimately less interesting kind of failure—the failure to try.' A and D are the viewpoint the author is arguing against. C is not mentioned.
